speaker: interviewer
text: It's S-C-H-W-A-R-T-Z. This is roll number 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Schwartz, you were telling me about the barracks in the camp in Riga.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were a little bit more comfortable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from what I remember, they had, which they used to call them latrines, like the-- like they have it here. And that was our facilities. We did have water. We did have sink with water. But we had no showers. So we used to call them [HUNGARIAN]. It means a cat wash in Hungarian. Because understand, I was with Hungarians. When I was in [INAUDIBLE] it was all Hungarians. So being I spoke the language, we used-- it was all Hungarian. So we used to sneak away and take paper towels wet and get ourselves washed up. That's one thing we had, who worked with us, a couple, which was from our people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's from-- what do you mean our people?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- well, [? if ?] there were a few older people, so they picked them that they were overseeing, that whatever we do, we are doing it right, that we are stand-- you know, we had a line. We have a work line. And the main thing what they-- they were stressing, if you-- if you stay clean, this will keep you alive, which before, we had to go to work, we had to get up and stay-- of course, there wasn't enough sinks. Some of them-- we had the sinks like they have it in the military, the big wash rooms.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went out and washed ourselves. We didn't have too many towels. So we had to use whatever we had. I don't remember if we had change of clothes or whatever. I just don't remember how we managed. What we had to do was at night, just rinse it out, and dry it, hang it on the bed overnight. It was still wet in the morning. You had no choice. You just had to put it on because that was it. What time of the year is this already?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the summer, which wasn't bad. And from there--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned before the beginning of the tape that you met you sister again.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where I'm going to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, good.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where I'm going to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah ha.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they shipped-- they took us down to the port. And we went by boat to Riga.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the port?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was Riga, as far as I remember. They put us also in a military boat, which was very bad. And they crowded us three, four in a bed. There wasn't enough water that it was so hot. There was, of course, no air conditioning, which the SS were all the time with us. But they tried to do the best that we-- that they-- that we could. They always allowed us like 10, 15 people to go up on the deck. And they let us stay there like, for 10 minutes. And they kept rotating us. Because quite a few people-- they fainted. They couldn't take the heat. And they took us to Riga. To-- from Riga, we went to Stutthof. At Stutthof, like I said, that's where I met my sister. But we weren't there very long. But the time I was there was enough. It was just the middle in the summer. It must have been July, or whatever. And we had to stay outside, wait for our food. There was a big line. They did not have-- will you believe it. They did not have enough plate for-- for dishes for the people. So the people who were first in line got their food. When they finished, they handed it next in line, which, of course, I was like, the middle on the line. And somebody in the front was finished the food, and she handed me a plate. And as it-- as it happened, two people ahead of me didn't have the plates. And there, we had already-- the overseeing us there were Russian women. They were very mean. They were just, for no reason at all, walking around and just hitting us for no reason at all. They just felt-- felt like it. The line isn't straight. You're not looking right. You are talking to somebody. I got such a beating because I took the plate instead of giving it for the lady in the front. As a matter of fact, I just had last year the [INAUDIBLE] a sonogram of my head, because I was [? hit ?] with a hose over the head. And of course, that day I didn't get any food. I was punished. With a beating I was punished, something which I'll never forget. But we did not stay there for very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just said that they were Russian, they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were also prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the ladies that were taking care of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were Russian ladies. How-- before you were with the Germans and--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain that a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were mixed. They were Russian camps. And there were Gypsy camps. So whoever wanted, or they wanted, or where they were forced to, they had to-- they-- they took 'em-- they gave them a choice. Do you want to work in the factory? Or do you want to oversee the people? So they took a [NON-ENGLISH] to oversee the people. Some of them had no choice. But they could have been nice. And they just could have been mean. It was hot. And they resented it, also. They had to stay on the heat all day long. They had to let it out to somebody. Who do you letting it out? To the Jewish. The Jewish people they blame. Because of us, they're suffering too, which we had nothing to do with it. Yeah, let me tell you a little story afterwards, relating to this why. Before I forget, let me tell you about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son was six years old, the older one. And he's asking me, mommy, why don't I have grandparents? I told him they died. Why? So I told him they died in the camps. Why were they in camp? Were they bad people? Did they steal something? Were they really robbers? Did they shot somebody? How do you explain a six-year-old kid that they were just there-- lost them because they were Jews. So it's the same thing, wherever we went. We were Jews. And we were punished for it. But they cannot pull us down enough. We are still jumping back from whatever we are doing. And there will always be Jewish people. And this is something after the war, after the war, I always-- whatever I did, I'm very proud to be Jewish. Well, now let's go back to Stutthof. Stutthof was [INAUDIBLE] on buses.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were beaten that day. And they didn't give you any food.

speaker: interviewee
text: No food.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at what time did you-- that's where you found your sister again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to tell me how you did? Or episode-- do you remember? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I saw my sister. I-- I-- I saw my sister behind a gate. And I tried to talk to her. And she told me on what barrack she was in. And I went to look for her in the daytime. But I couldn't find it, because I wanted to arrange that evening to-- to exchange her with one of my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you saw her when you got out.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got it, we were like on one end, maybe. I don't know how far-- pretty far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's off the boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were already in the camp. At Stutthof was a very big camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get from the boat to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: By trucks. So we got off-- they took us in the camp. Once the gate-- the gates closed, behind the wire, where they made us-- made us get off the camp. And once we got off, and we just got off the trucks. And I looked far away. There were people staying-- who is coming in? They were not working. It wasn't a working camp, Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it wasn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stutthof wasn't-- it was just a trans-- a transport camp, or whatever you call it. And she was staying by the fence. And I recognized her from very far away, just by standing. I recognized her. And I told my people, this is my sister. And they said, don't go near the fence. Because if you touch it, it's electric. Don't go near it. But they couldn't stop me. I says, I don't care what it is. I got to make sure. I got to talk to her. But in the daytime, I don't know if there was electricity in the camp or not. But I remember, I had some bread on me. Because we were given some bread when we got off the boat. So I threw it over the fence, to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- did she recognize you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh yes. Oh, she saw me right away. She did. And then she told me what happened to my sisters and to my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you could talk to her at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I could talk to her from far away-- not for very long, because they made me go back. But for a little while, we talked-- long enough till the SS men came and, go back in line. You cannot stay here. But it was just enough to get information, which at that time, I was satisfied, till after the war, I found out what real story was. Believe me, she was only a kid-- maybe 14. And she wouldn't tell me the real story. She did not want to hurt me. She tried to cover it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you saw her, and you had a couple of words with her, what happened after?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to a-- we went to the barracks and didn't do nothing, just waiting. Because we had to get up to appear in the morning-- [INAUDIBLE]. And if they decided they wanted to keep us--

speaker: interviewer
text: I need you to talk a little bit louder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Louder? All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they felt like they wanted to count us, make sure we were all there. We had to stay. They took us out. It didn't bother them if they let us stay in the hot sun for two hours. So-- but this, we already took it for granted. We were already used to that. So that's part of-- part of the things. Well, from there, we-- they took us to Glewe, which was also in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: I [? will ?] go--

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like-- yeah. I would like to hear more the details with your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, with my sister, I spoke to her. And she did not know about my plan. Because I went looking for her. See, we were in one camp. And she was in the other one. And there was a gate. And there was a gate. And there was somebody staying by the gate. And I did go to the gate. And I asked her-- I think it was one of ours watching the gate. And I'd ask her, I says, look. My sister is here. I like to go in to find her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she told me, go ahead. But come back before dark, because then when the light goes on, and the electricity goes on, you won't be able to go back. So I did go back. And she told me at what-- where she was staying. Because there was quite a few-- how do you call it-- barracks. So I went into the barrack, which I thought she told me she was in. And I yelled. And I looked. And she wasn't there. And-- But there were other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: As it happens, there was somebody else, one of them. And she didn't know. She didn't know anybody by that name. And of course, I had to go back. So I didn't go searching. I was just hoping that the next day she was going to come and I will see here again. Meanwhile, when I came back, I met up with a friend of mine. Because her-- she found out that her sister was over there, also. But we were going to exchange. She was going to stay there with her sister. And I was going to take mine. Unfortunately, it didn't come true. Because we left. I never heard from her again. I looked. I put in names after the war-- names, looking. I went home. But so far, whatever happened, it has been-- they just weren't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: That my-- that was my younger sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here name was Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sarah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Sora. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after? Where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take us to another working camp, Glewe. There, we worked, also, being that we were young and strong. So we got a transport, got into a transport company, which there were riding 20 of us. And we had a couple, which she was terrific. She was an actress. And she already was in-- she was already sent to-- to Poland. Because before that, they took the people to Poland somewhere, not to Auschwitz yet. I mean, not the camps-- that she was able to escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was very good to us. She knew how to handle the SS women. Because we had SS women who watched us. So during lun-- or during lunch time, or whatever, when they had lunch, they took-- we had some of us who was a beautician-- used to wash her hair, and make her hair, make her nails, so they were soft. So they were a little bit nicer to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was also, when we went to work in that company, which I'm never going to forget him-- he was a Wehrmacht, an older man who was watching us. He felt very bad for us. And this was already in the fall. It was already snow outside. The ground was frozen. And we had to dig ditches, carry [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to build the railroad tracks. We had to carry bricks, like for five, six buildings up. They were extending building-- buildings. They were building something with bricks, extending the buildings. We had to carry up buildings. But we made a chain. And we were towing the bricks up there. Because we were a-- we were a young group and we were able to do it. We also had to move around the bombs, missiles. We had to get in from one-- from one place to the other. But they told us all, it's dead. So don't worry about it. But going back to that old man, to the Wehrmacht, he was a doll. They did not give him for them too much to eat. That's all what he had was a sandwich and an apple for lunch. He ate a half a sandwich. The other half a sandwich, he cut up, like six to eight pieces, and gave each of us a little bite. He did the same thing with the apple. He took a half an apple, and he gave us the half to everybody. And every day, he gave to a different group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I don't. Think it was Fritz or something. Who knew the name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They wouldn't tell us the name. And he taught us some songs while we were marching in the woods, because it was very cold. And he says, [SINGING GERMAN]. Today we will start to learn a new march. And songs go so and so. He kept us-- he felt very bad for us, but there was nothing he could do. [COUGHING] And when-- [COUGHING] --and when he was with us that day-- that day, and he was-- he allowed us to make a fire outside, in one of the garbage cans. So he made like that. Somebody always had to keep moving. So by the time we got back with carrying the things for the train, the tracks-- so you put down the tracks. And we were watching till the other one come. We could stay like, five minutes and warm up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were lucky that we were frostbitten, but not that bad. Because quite a few of us came back with frostbitten legs, frostbitten hands, because we had no gloves. We just took some rags and wrapped around our hands. And you couldn't-- you couldn't even get them. Where do you get those things? They didn't give it to us. What did you eat at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us-- in the morning, we had black coffee. And the evening, they gave us a bowl of soup, and they gave us potatoes in the skins, most of them-- potatoes and beets boiled-- excuse me-- boiled in the skins, and also, a dark bread. It must have been like, two slic-- like, two slices of bread. So we kind of saved from the evening. We had the soup and part of, I don't know, two or three potatoes we had. So we saved them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the potatoes, we used to take along with us for lunch and heat it up on the-- on the open fire. And you ate. And of course, then when we had also-- the Germans, during lunch hour, they had a picnic. They needed something else. So they had no choice. They just entertained themselves. They just go, picked up one or two girls, and they had to go with them. There was just no other way. But I was lucky. Because I was a dumpy little kid. So I got away with it. But at night, we had it also pretty good comparison the other people. Because we had barracks, and we had beds. And we had to get up before going to work. Even now, it-- can you imagine, in the winter, going out with a blanket over you and taking a cold shower? But we did it. And that kept us going. And we were-- that's-- there it was the longest time. How long was it? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I'm trying to think. It must have been July, August, September, October, November. I-- I really can't remember. But we were there a [? nice-- ?] at least six-- six months, I think, we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you go-- when you went out in the fields, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were digging ditches for bunkers. We were, like I said, building new tracks for the trains. If they needed those things or not, or just to keep us busy, I don't know. If there was a train to unload, we would [INAUDIBLE] unpack it, just to do it. If there were cars to load, we were there-- packages, whatever the-- what got in

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was only girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. In Glewe there was no men at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the living conditions at-- in--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were pretty good. Because each of us had our own bed. And we had-- like I said, the outside was the latrine. Then there were the showers. And there was enough showers to go out in the morning and first taking a shower. At night, there were-- the time was our own, except for some of us. They had to go in to entertain the Germans. They just came and they picked the girls, whoever they felt like it, and took them away to their barracks. So sometimes, they had to say there overnight. But this-- you had nothing, nothing to do. But we had a very smart block. And [? Eltha-- ?] she was the black, which she was also ours. She was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was our, also. But she was just all that. She was a schoolteacher before that. And she knew a little German. We had there also a sick house. And-- What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you call it-- infirmary. Fact, I wind up with temperature. And I didn't go to work. She came in the evening, and she looked around, and she says, you know something? By tomorrow morning, I want everybody out. Whoever remains here, they're coming in today, and they will take you away. Which, of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she know?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was something we were also lucky. The German SS man who was the whole-- over the whole camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The president. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember names. He was born in Hungary. But their parents moved to Germany. So he was a German soldier. He even spoke a little bit Hungarian. And being she was nice with him-- not that she had a choice-- to send over the girls, the girls at night, so they did tell her in advance that somebody is coming. Because we had there a young girl who gave birth. Of course, they took her away to Auschwitz, whatever. Never saw her again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- regardless if you have temperature, don't say anything. Just get out. And you go to work, if you want to live. So it was still-- we managed. Let's put it this way. Where were you at the time that they told you, go out and work? At the infirmary?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the infirmary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, from the infirmary.

speaker: interviewee
text: To go back to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah ha.

speaker: interviewee
text: To go back-- go back to the barracks, go back to your group, and go back to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe infirmary for me a little?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was also like they had-- like-- I don't remember-- just two, two beds, one above the other. And the room wasn't too big. There was one, two, three, four-- maybe six or eight beds. And then the nurses had a little room, a little tiny office. It wasn't too big. But was in the corner and it was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had medicines, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. That's all what I remember. And I remember taking the-- taking my temperature, because I don't run temperature. I can be very sick and I don't run any temperature. But she looked at me, says, you haven't got too much temperature. But from what I see, you are sick. There were also our [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], our nurses, also from-- [INAUDIBLE]. You know, they had all kind of people there. They had people-- professors, doctors, nurses, all of them. And some of them had a chance to practice their professions. So they helped us out. Of course, some of them worked with the Germans, which they didn't know on whose side to be on. It was tough, tough decision, what they had to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did young girls, like you at that time, entertain themselves? Or the free-- if you can call that free time--

speaker: interviewee
text: Believe it or not, there was a group, which I had seen in [? Cosov ?] they had, when I was there-- actresses. And she put up-- they got together, and they put on a show. It was unbelievable. I don't remember the show-- which one, what the name of it. But she got together in the evening. And they-- they-- it was there was no music. It was just all talk. Because we didn't have any music. But we had the run of the barracks. We all got together. And they entertained. She was just terrific. It was a funny show. I don't remember the name of it. But she also played from a family, which they're separated. And she played that, finally, an 80-year-old woman, a young girl-- I mean, the way-- we had to get a make up, to make her up. I'm never going to remember to what extent, because I had seen it in the theater. And she was made up like an 80-year-old woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there she was sitting, with all her riches. She had all the riches under her feet in the-- in one of the boxes, the strong box. She carried it with her whatever she went. Finally, they tried to take it away from her. And she wouldn't give it up-- I mean, that's part of the play. Finally, and she says, what do you want, my riches or me? So I think the son came over, finally, went to. So we didn't have time for entertainment. Who was thinking of entertainment? And we came back after a day's work. And you were very happy to relax. Because 4 o'clock the next morning, you had to get up. It was dark outside when you had to get up. We're going to change a tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
